schools.summary: Allegorise the hours as ages of the world or of human life, the denarius as eternal life, the vineyard as the Church; Augustine adds 'many mansions' within the one reward.
The kingdom as a matter of rewards; rooted in the rich young ruler episode and the disciples' 'What shall we have?'; vineyard from Isaiah 5:7.
Calvin curbs the allegory: the sole point is that the first have no right to boast; God may call latecomers and make them equal.
Origen: the present life is one day, the marketplace all outside the Church, the denarius salvation.
Henry holds both the Jew/Gentile sense and the general truth that God is debtor to no man.
Wesley reads a primary (Jews rejected, Gentiles accepted) and secondary (order of reward) scope.

debates.question: Should the parable's details be allegorised (hours, denarius, etc.) or read for one point only?

sides.position: Allegorise the ages/stages and the call of the Gentiles
Read for the single point that the first have no right to boast

outliers.note: Reconciles the one denarius with 'many mansions' — equal eternal life but diverse degrees of glory.

schools.summary: The penny as the Roman denarius, a just day's wage; Lightfoot distinguishes day-hire from hour-hire from the rabbinic canons.
Gill notes these were labourers 'hired for a day'.

outliers.note: The note here concerns the opinions about Jesus in Matthew 14, not this verse.

schools.summary: The third hour as nine a.m.; the marketplace as the place where the unemployed waited to be hired; Ellicott sees the later calls picturing Matthew and others summoned after the first disciples.
No fixed wage with the later labourers but trust in the householder's equity; a little work done in trust better than much in the hireling's spirit (Ellicott).
Gill: a call of free grace out of the world into the church, to the weak and base things of the world (1 Corinthians 1:27).
Gill: the third, sixth and ninth hours were the Jewish hours of prayer; the householder keeps going forth to call, giving the same promise.
Clarke fixes the sixth hour at noon and the ninth at three in the afternoon.
The eleventh hour as five p.m., one hour left; Ellicott hints the first labourers worked less zealously; Robertson on 'the problem of the unemployed'.
Gill reads the hours as ages of the world, gospel dispensations, or stages of life, the eleventh hour the aged and the Gentiles.
Wesley: the eleventh hour means very late, long after the rest.
The latecomers' idleness was no fault of theirs; they obeyed at once in full faith (Ellicott); Clarke notes the textual doubt over the last clause.
Gill applies the excuse to the Gentiles, long neglected until the gospel was sent to the nations.
The steward and the prompt payment by Mosaic law (Deuteronomy 24:15); the last-to-first order needed to make the murmuring possible.
Calvin: seek no mystery in the order — it only sets up the murmuring; men have no right to complain of God's bounty to the unworthy.

debates.question: Does paying the last first teach that the temporally last are crowned above the first?

sides.position: No — it is only a device to occasion the murmuring

outliers.note: The note here concerns Peter and the keys (Matthew 16), not this verse.

schools.summary: The full wage to one-hour men as generosity not injustice; the standard qualitative not quantitative; the penny as eternal life (Ellicott).
Gill: the eleventh-hour men are the aged, the Gentiles, or the last of the elect, receiving the same inheritance by free grace.
Wesley holds both senses — the Gentiles, or any called late in life.
The first expected more by length of labour; Ellicott sees the disciples' own thoughts (Matthew 19:27) mirrored; the paid wage softens the severity.
Gill reads the first as the earliest saints or first-believing Jews.
Wesley: the first are the Jews expecting precedence over the Gentiles.
'Goodman of the house' corrected to 'householder'; the verb an onomatopoetic, prolonged 'began to grumble'.
Clarke: the Jews made the call of the Gentiles a pretext to reject the gospel (Acts 11, 15).
Gill: the murmurers receive only the external privileges of the dispensation.
The burden and heat as the scorching sirocco (Genesis 41:6; Jonah 4:8; Ezekiel 17:10); the labourers 'seemed to have a good case'.
Wesley ties 'made them equal' to Acts 15:9 — God put no difference between Jew and Gentile.
Gill: the equal pay for one hour was grudged by the Jews.

themes: burden and heat of the day

schools.summary: 'Friend' carries reproof (cf. Matthew 22:12; 26:50); the householder kept the contract and may freely do as he wills with the rest; no disciple should grudge grace to the penitent thief.
Clarke: the call of the Gentiles is no impediment to the Jews — room enough in heaven for all.
The Cyril of Jerusalem excerpt meditates on the penitent thief and the swiftness of grace, tangential to the verse.

outliers.note: A catechetical passage on the penitent thief, touching the theme of mercy to the latecomer rather than expounding the verse.

schools.summary: 'Take what is justly yours' and the sovereign 'what I will'; Ellicott on whether the murmurers forfeit the reward — heaven a state of harmony with God, not a place.
Gill: the dismissal pictures nominal Jewish professors from whom the gospel was taken.
Wesley: the sovereign will to give the last (Gentiles, publicans, sinners) as much as the first.

outliers.note: The note here concerns taking up the cross (Mark 8:34), not this verse.

schools.summary: The 'evil eye' as the envious, malicious eye (Deuteronomy 15:9; Proverbs 23:6); the householder responsible to God only, free to give of his own.
Gill: gifts, grace and glory are God's own, bestowed by sovereign pleasure (Romans 9:15-24).
Wesley limits the sovereignty to bounty — it cannot warrant reprobating an unborn soul; the rebuke is of envy.

debates.question: How far does 'do what I will with mine own' extend God's sovereignty?

sides.position: To the free bestowal of grace and glory on whom he will
Only to bounty, not to reprobating the unborn

outliers.note: A catechetical passage on the penitent thief, tangential to the verse.

schools.summary: Barnes narrows it to degrees of reward within the church; Ellicott to degrees in the life to come by temper not duration; both note 'few chosen' is text-critically doubtful here.
Calvin: not Jew/Gentile nor elect/reprobate, but a call to run with alacrity and share the prize modestly.
Clarke and Wesley keep the Jew/Gentile sense; 'called' = all who hear, 'chosen' = those who obey.

debates.question: What is the scope of 'the last first'?

sides.position: Degrees of reward within the church, not election or Gentiles
The call of the Gentiles over the Jews
A general call to humble diligence

schools.summary: Chrysostom: the prediction is private lest the multitude be confounded; Jesus comes to his passion wittingly and willingly; Pseudo-Chrysostom: to be willing to die is more than human.
Jerome and Raban: he fortifies them against the scandal of the cross; Augustine meditates on the two deaths and one resurrection.
The setting after Lazarus and Ephraim; Mark's note that Jesus went before them, amazed and afraid (Mark 10:32) — the shadow of the cross.
Calvin: he repeats the prediction to exhort them to steadiness, fortifying them by foreknowledge and the prophets.
Henry: it is good to think and speak often of our death — a way of dying daily.
The stages of the prediction — betrayal, Sanhedrin's condemnation, delivery to the Gentiles — foretold in Isaiah 53 and Daniel 9:26; new definiteness over Matthew 17:22.
Calvin: the minute foreknowledge proves his divinity; he willingly hastens to death yet candidly warns the disciples and points to the resurrection.
Cyril of Jerusalem (on point): Christ came to his passion willingly, lest the world perish; the Cross is his glory.
The Gentile agents — Herod's mockery, Pilate's scourging and crucifixion; the prophecies (Isaiah 53:5; Acts 3:13); the resurrection clause hidden from the disciples (Luke 18:34).
The mother as Salome, possibly the Lord's aunt, prompting her sons; an inopportune request fixed on the twelve thrones (Matthew 19:28; Luke 19:11).
Jerome on her womanish eagerness; Pseudo-Chrysostom defends the heavenly aim but faults the ignorance; Augustine harmonises with Mark 10:35.
Calvin: a mirror of human vanity — holy zeal corrupted by ambition; the brothers put their mother forward, conscience reproving them.
Origen: womanish simplicity and the brothers' imperfect notions of Christ's kingdom.
Henry: whom Christ loves best he reproves most.

debates.question: Did the mother or the sons make the request?

sides.position: The sons, through their mother (harmonising with Mark 10:35)

schools.summary: They sought the highest places in a temporal kingdom (1 Kings 2:19); Clarke links it to the twelve thrones and the Sanhedrin arrangement; Ellicott notes the favour already shown them.
Calvin: a noble faith in Christ's kingdom corrupted by ambition and a non-existent earthly picture; pray that faith be kept pure.
Wesley: still they expected a temporal kingdom.
The cup as affliction (Isaiah 51:17; Psalm 75:8), the baptism as overwhelming calamity (Psalm 42:7; Luke 12:50); the baptism clause text-critically doubtful; 'We are able' proves their ignorance.
Calvin: Christ corrects ambition by holding out the cross — none shares his kingdom who has not shared his sufferings; their answer is the presumption of the flesh.
Clarke applies it to coveting church office; Wesley: all who share his kingdom must first share his sufferings.
Cyril of Jerusalem (on point): martyrdom is a baptism in one's own blood.

debates.question: Is the clause 'baptized with the baptism' part of the original text here?

sides.position: Probably not — absent from the best MSS, added from Mark

schools.summary: The italicised 'it shall be given' perverts the sense — read 'except to those for whom it is prepared' (cf. Matthew 25:34); James martyred (Acts 12:2), John exiled (Revelation 1:9).
Calvin: Christ surrenders nothing but says the Father had not assigned him to allot each one's place; be wise with sobriety (1 John 3:2).
Wesley: it is his to give in strictest propriety, but only to those prepared — who endure in faith working by love.
Cyril of Jerusalem repeats the martyr-baptism theme.

debates.question: Does 'not mine to give' deny Christ's power to bestow the places?

sides.position: No — the KJV insertion distorts it; he gives them, but to those prepared by the Father
The Father, not the Son, assigns each one's place

schools.summary: The ten's anger was the jealousy of rivals, not righteous zeal (Ellicott); ambition for spiritual lordship has ruined churches (Clarke).
Chrysostom: they vented grief nursed since the Transfiguration; Pseudo-Chrysostom: the ten grieve as carnally as the two asked.
Calvin: their displeasure was ill-founded; Christ exposes a disease lurking in all of them.
Origen contrasts oppressive worldly rulers with Christ's order.
The Gentile rulers 'lord it over' their subjects (1 Peter 5:3); Christ's kingdom has no ranks of dominion; 'minister' = deacon, servant; Clarke warns against mixing politics and church.
Calvin distinguishes the Church's government from worldly empire — pastors serve, not rule; guards against the Anabaptist exclusion of magistrates.
Wesley: they imagined the chief would lord it as the Gentiles, but it is quite otherwise.
Jesus does not condemn the desire for greatness but redirects it — the way to be first is to serve; service is itself the highest greatness (Robertson, Ellicott).
Calvin: self-love is to be turned to a new object — to submit to the brethren; Gill: the saying does not abolish church order but levels the apostles among themselves.
Clarke's fivefold portrait of the true minister — not lording, serving by loving instruction, ready to die for the flock.
'Servant' (slave) is lower than 'minister', 'chief' higher than 'great'; the greatness IS the service, not a reward deferred to the next life (Ellicott).
Clarke: the minister is not a lord over the flock but a servant ready to lay down his life.
Gill glosses 'chief' as the first place in the Messiah's kingdom.
The ransom (lutron) as the price for captives (Exodus 21:30; Leviticus 25:50), Christ dying in the sinner's place; 'anti' carries exchange/substitution (Robertson against evasions).
Calvin: Christ emptied himself (Philippians 2:7), his life the price of redemption, overturning merit; 'many' contrasts the one with all (Romans 5:15).
The Cyril of Jerusalem entry cites Daniel's Son of man (Daniel 7:13).

debates.question: Does 'a ransom for many' teach substitutionary atonement?

sides.position: Yes — Christ died in the place of sinners (anti = exchange)

schools.summary: Harmonise the one blind man (Bartimaeus) with Matthew's two and Luke's 'came nigh' (Luke 18:35); old and new Jericho; Jericho's history (Joshua 6:26; 1 Kings 16:34).
Pseudo-Chrysostom and Chrysostom: spiritual love feels no fatigue; faith quickened by being forbidden; Christ let them be hindered to prove their desire.
Augustine and others allegorise the two blind men as Israel and the Gentiles, or souls hindered by the worldly crowd; Jerome/Raban as Pharisees and Sadducees, Jericho the changeful world.
Henry: joint prayer pleases Christ (Matthew 18:19); place yourself where Christ passes.
Origen reads the two blind men as Judah and Israel, blind to the true word of Law and Prophets.
Mark/Luke name only Bartimaeus; Gill posits three blind men; Ellicott allows Luke may be more accurate; 'Son of David' the popular messianic title (cf. Matthew 15:22).
Calvin: they honour Christ by seeking relief and confess him Messiah, Son of David, the only Author of salvation.
Origen gathers the titles 'Son of David' and 'Son of God' to show the differences of those who come to Christ.
Wesley: Bartimaeus, the more eminent, spoke for both.

debates.question: How are the differing Gospel accounts (one/two men, entering/leaving Jericho) reconciled?

sides.position: Mark/Luke name the conspicuous one; Matthew counts two
There were three blind men in all

schools.summary: The crowd rebuked them as troublesome; Jesus had them called and Bartimaeus cast off his garment; the Kyrie Eleïson modelled on their cry (Ellicott).
Clarke and Wesley: the world drowns the soul's cry, but the rebuke proves salvation is nigh — cry the more, or come short of a cure.
Calvin: even Christ's followers may hinder our approach; perseverance and the redoubled cry overcome every obstacle.
Christ's standing still shows his regard for faith and prayer; Mark's 'Be of good cheer' and Bartimaeus casting off his cloak; the question asked with the insight of sympathy.
Calvin: Christ asks though resolved to grant, since they prayed by a special movement of the Spirit; the question awakens the crowd to observe the miracle.
They ask sight, not alms — the afflicted heart tells its own sorrow; Mark preserves 'Rabboni' (cf. John 20:16).
Gill: asking sight not alms shows faith in his power; opening blind eyes was a prophesied Messianic work (Isaiah 35:5).
The verb of tender compassion and the soothing touch; 'thy faith hath saved thee' — faith led them to Jesus; the marks of an undoubted public miracle (Barnes).
Calvin: the following is gratitude — the healed become disciples, mirrors of Christ's grace confirming the miracle.
Clarke's appeal: act for your soul as they did for their sight, for Christ is passing by.
